coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as biocompatible fluorescent agents for labelling human red blood cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ristić, Biljana
Trpkov, Đorđe
Dojčilović, Radovan
Đukić, Tamara
Božanić, Dušan K.
Stefanović, Radoš
Vasić, Borislav
Drvenica, Ivana
coredata.dc:description: Evaluating the biocompatibility of nanoparticles with blood is essential to demonstrate their biosafety, reduce potential adverse effects, and enable their application in nanomedicine. Although many studies have explored interactions between blood and nanomaterials, only limited number have specifically addressed the compatibility of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CD) with red blood cells (RBC), the most abundant cells in blood, that are essential for healthy functioning of all vertebrates through their role in oxygen transport. This study investigated the biological properties of several concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL) of negatively charged, green fluorescent N-CD, synthesized using environmentally friendly precursors through a hydrothermal method, on healthy human RBC in vitro. Scanning electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy revealed that the treatment with N-CD, even at the highest concentration, did not significantly affect RBC morphology. Interfacial interaction between N-CD and RBC was demonstrated by photoluminescence spectroscopy, fluorescence microscopy, and synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. The treatment with N-CD at the highest concentration had no effects on the RBC osmotic fragility, slightly increased the RBC deformability, and demonstrated a noticeable protective effect on the RBC hemolysis after 24 h. Flow cytometry analysis confirmed that N-CD did not alter the RBC morphology and did not result in an increase in the production of reactive oxygen species or reactive nitrogen species, indicating that their interaction did not lead to oxidative stress induction in human RBC. These findings suggest that fluorescent N-CD are biocompatible, fluorescent RBC imaging agents and hold promise as candidates for developing novel RBC-based drug delivery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Erythrocytes
Nanomaterials
Hemocompatibility
Hemolysis
Deformability
Morphology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the hemocompatibility effect of N-CD on RBC
Interfacial interaction of N-CD by RBC
Flow cytometry analysis of RBC morphology and intracellular ROS production
The effect of N-CD on RBC morphology
Analysis of interfacial interaction between N-CD and RBC
Impact of N-CD on induction of RBC oxidative stress
Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge funding provided by the Institute for Medical Research, University of Belgrade, (contract No. 451-03-136/2025-03/200015), the Institute of Physics Belgrade, and Vin\u010Da Institute of Nuclear Sciences (contract No. 451\u201303\u2013136/2025\u201303/200017), through the grants from the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia.

The authors acknowledge funding provided by the Institute for Medical Research, University of Belgrade , (contract No. 451-03-66/2024-03/200015 ), the Institute of Physics Belgrade , and Vin\u010Da Institute of Nuclear Science (contract No. 451\u201303\u201366/2024\u201303/200017 ), through the grants from the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CD: The representative AFM images of (a) as prepared N-CD and (c) N-CD upon dispersion in PBS with the corresponding height distributions of the particles given in (b) and (d), respectively. The DLS size distribution graph of N-CD dispersed in PBS (e). Photoluminescence emission (solid line) and excitation (dotted lines) spectra of N-CD PBS dispersion (f).

The effect of N-CD on RBC morphology. The RBC morphology after incubation with PBS or with N-CD in PBS (200 μg/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C in the dark was assessed by: FE-SEM (a,b) and AFM (c,d,e), respectively. 3D and 2D topographic image of RBC and RBC with adsorbed N-CD and corresponding height profiles along the dashed line (c,d,e). Topographic image of the square domain encircled by the dashed line in (d) is displayed in (e). The flatten surface of the middle image from (e) was obtained by subtracting the second-order surface from the parabolic surface of the left image from (e). Representative micrographs from two independent experiment are shown.

Interaction of N-CD with RBC. PL emission spectra of supernatants (a) and pellets (b) obtained by separating RBC and RBC incubated with N-CD from the initial PBS dispersions. Fluorescence images of RBC and RBC incubated with N-CD; the scale bar is 10 μm (c).
Synchronous fluorescence maps of (a) RBC and (b) NCD/RBC sample. (c) Synchronous fluorescence spectra of RBC and N-CD samples.

The effects of N-CD on RBC hemolysis, deformability, and osmotic fragility. RBC are incubated with PBS in the presence or absence of N-CD at different concentrations (25–200 μg/mL) for 2-24 h (a,b) or 2 h (c, d) in the dark. The RBC hemolysis was assessed by hemolytic activity test (a, b). The representative photograph is shown (a). The extent of hemolysis (%) of RBC incubated with N-CD for 2–24 h at 37 °C (b). The representative osmotic fragility curves (fitted by a Boltzmann sigmoidal function) (c), and the deformability curves of RBC treated with N-CD after 2 h of treatment (d).

Flow cytometry analysis of RBC after 6 h of incubation with different concentrations of N-CD (50–200 μg/mL). The fluorescence intensity of DCFDA, DAF and DHE-stained cells as a measure of ROS, RNS and O2 •− production were examined by flow cytometry. The representative boxplots of DCFDA, DAF and DHE fluorescence (a, b, c) presented fluorescence intensity obtained from two independent experiments.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as biocompatible fluorescent agents for labelling human red blood cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Evaluating the biocompatibility of nanoparticles with blood is essential to demonstrate their biosafety, reduce potential adverse effects, and enable their application in nanomedicine. Although many studies have explored interactions between blood and nanomaterials, only limited number have specifically addressed the compatibility of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CD) with red blood cells (RBC), the most abundant cells in blood, that are essential for healthy functioning of all vertebrates through their role in oxygen transport. This study investigated the biological properties of several concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL) of negatively charged, green fluorescent N-CD, synthesized using environmentally friendly precursors through a hydrothermal method, on healthy human RBC in vitro. Scanning electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy revealed that the treatment with N-CD, even at the highest concentration, did not significantly affect RBC morphology. Interfacial interaction between N-CD and RBC was demonstrated by photoluminescence spectroscopy, fluorescence microscopy, and synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. The treatment with N-CD at the highest concentration had no effects on the RBC osmotic fragility, slightly increased the RBC deformability, and demonstrated a noticeable protective effect on the RBC hemolysis after 24 h. Flow cytometry analysis confirmed that N-CD did not alter the RBC morphology and did not result in an increase in the production of reactive oxygen species or reactive nitrogen species, indicating that their interaction did not lead to oxidative stress induction in human RBC. These findings suggest that fluorescent N-CD are biocompatible, fluorescent RBC imaging agents and hold promise as candidates for developing novel RBC-based drug delivery systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request. All data are securely backed up on the institutional cloud storage platform (Microsoft OneDrive), with restricted access.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) are three-dimensional nanosized carbon-based materials that can be produced by simple synthetic routes using abundant and low-cost sources. CD have attracted considerable research and technological interest in recent decades due to their diverse and favorable physicochemical properties, including their excellent photostability and tunable photoluminescence. Moreover, the inherent water solubility, appreciable biocompatibility, and low toxicity of CD make them promising candidates for biomedical applications such as bioimaging, photodynamic or photothermal therapy, and drug/gene delivery [1–4]. The versatility of CD' biological activities is facilitated by their easy surface modification during synthesis, including functionalization with various surface groups. Doping CD with heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen, sulfur) allows the effective tuning of their intrinsic characteristics while simultaneously improving their biological properties, thereby expanding their potential for novel biomedical applications [5,6]. Nitrogen is one of the most used elements for doping CD since its sizeable atomic radius to carbon allows its efficient incorporation into the sp2 graphitic or pyridinic structure of CD. In comparison to carbon, nitrogen also has an additional pair of electrons in the valence band, which can contribute to the chemical bonding of the CD and can readily be transported to the orbitals of sp2 carbon structures [7]. In that sense, it has been shown that nitrogen doping alters the electronic band structure of CD, enhancing their optical absorption and emission characteristics and significantly improving their photoluminescence properties. Consequently, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CD) represent efficient nanomaterials for bioimaging applications that allow for clearer and more precise visualization of biological tissues and cells [8,9]. The surface functionalization of CD can also facilitate and control drug loading and release, enhancing drug delivery efficiency to target sites in the body [10,11]. Moreover, N-CD can be used as biosensors for detection of specific biomolecules, pathogens, or changes in the cellular environment, making them useful for early disease diagnosis [7,12].

Most of the proposed administration routes of CD in biomedicine require direct contact with target tissue, finally reaching the bloodstream where they can interact with blood cells. Therefore, evaluating the biocompatibility of N-CD with blood cells is crucial to ensure the safety and compatibility of these nanoparticles with biological systems, to suppress unwanted effects such as disruptive interactions with plasma proteins, hemolysis, coagulation, or thrombus formation [13]. This is a particularly relevant aspect for erythrocytes (red blood cells, RBC), the most abundant cellular component of the blood (>99 %), which are essential for delivery of oxygen to the tissues. The specialized structure of the RBC membrane, which combines internal viscosity with elasticity of cytoskeleton, gives RBC high flexibility and allows them to deform and pass through narrow capillaries while also providing resistance to shear stress. Therefore, the deformability of RBC is a crucial biomechanical property to maintain proper blood flow in the microcirculation [14,15]. Due to their high abundance and ease of handling, RBC serve as an excellent model for preliminary investigations of the complex interactions between mammalian cell membranes and external biomaterials [16].

Although there are many studies investigating blood-nanomaterial interactions, to the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have thus far examined the hemocompatibility of chemically modified nanoparticles N-CD and their effects on red blood cells [17,18]. Additionally, examining the interaction between RBC and N-CD is crucial, as asserted by recent reports on the development of advanced RBC membrane-coated nanocarriers [19]. These bionic carriers exhibit unique biological and physical properties, such as immune evasion, prolonged circulation, and even the potential to cross the blood-brain barrier [20–22]. In the present study, we investigated for the first time the RBC hemocompatibility with newly synthetized N-CD [23] and elucidated the effects of this specific nanomaterial on RBC.

Here, we report that N-CD nanoparticles prepared by hydrothermal method from environmentally friendly precursors demonstrated a significant protective effect against RBC hemolysis, did not affect RBC morphology, showing no impact on osmotic fragility or reduction in RBC deformability. Furthermore, N-CD were partially internalized by RBC, with no significant impact on RBC morphology or induction of oxidative stress, while emitting bright green fluorescence under blue light excitation.

N-CD were synthesized using a bottom-up approach, reproducing the procedure that was described previously [23]. Briefly, N-CD solutions were prepared via a hydrothermal method, eco-friendly, low cost, and fast technique, from citric acid (CA, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) and urea (Kemika, Croatia, Zagreb). A mixture of CA and urea in molar ratio of 1:3 was dissolved in 12.5 mL of deionized water and placed in the oven at 180 °C for 4 h. The resulting dark syrup was then dispersed in deionized water. The stock solution of N-CD was obtained as the supernatant after centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The concentration of the N-CD solution (c = 8.5 mg/mL) was determined from the mass of the remaining powder after drying. Liquid and powder samples were stored at room temperature, where powder samples are suitable for long-term storage to preserve the N-CD quality. The preparation of N-CD is schematically presented in Supplementary material (Scheme 1).

For height and lateral size measurements of N-CD, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was employed after spin-coating of the synthesized N-CD on Si slipcovers using a Quesant AFM (Agoura Hills, CA). Additionally, AFM was used to examine the morphology and surface roughness of RBC after treatment with N-CD (200 μg/mL in isotonic 10 mM phosphate buffer saline with 0,8 % NaCl, pH 7.2–7.4 (PBS)) for 2 h. The RBC control sample, N-CD suspension in PBS and RBC sample treated with N-CD were fixed onto mica coverslips by incubation with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde in PBS (Glutaraldehyde, 50 % in water, Fluka Chemie AG, Buchs, Switzerland) for 1 h. The samples were then fixed with 0.1 % osmium tetroxide in PBS (4 % osmium tetroxide in H2O, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 1 h and washed twice with PBS. Sample drying was conducted using increasing concentrations of ethanol (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 95 % and 100 %) (absolute ethanol 99.9 %, Etanol Lab d.o.o., Šabac, Serbia). Topographic AFM measurements were performed in tapping contact mode at ambient conditions using the NTEGRA Prima system from NT-MDT (NT-MDT Co. Moscow, Russia). NSG01 silicone probes from NT-MDT, with a standard tip curvature radius of about 6 nm and a force constant of 5 N/m, were used for imaging. Topographic images were processed using instrument-equipped software through plane subtraction or by fitting with the first order lines. The height profile of N-CD was generated directly by instrument software (Quesant software).

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to determine the average particle size (hydrodynamic diameter, Dh) of the N-CD suspended in PBS at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. DLS measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK), equipped with a 10 mW He-Ne laser (scattering wavelength: λ = 633 nm). All DLS measurements were conducted at a single scattering angle (θ) of 173° and at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C (solvent: PBS, refractive index of the solvent: n = 1.33), using disposable polystyrene cuvettes. The values of Dh were obtained as the maximum peak of the number size distribution. For each sample, the measurement was repeated ten times with an integration time of 2 s. Additionally, ζ potential values of N-CD were determined using the same analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer, Malvern Instruments, UK) at 25 °C and θ = 12.8°, utilizing folded capillary cells (DTS 1060) and reported as an average of three measurements. The Zetasizer software (version 7.11, Malvern Instruments, UK) was used for data processing

The Horiba LabRam Soleil Raman microscope was used to acquire Raman signal from N-CD deposited on the glass slide. The excitation wavelength was set to 532 nm with 100× objective and power of 0.1 mW. The detection range was configured between 1000 and 2400 cm−1. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements of N-CD hydrocolloids, as well as of control RBC and RBC incubated with N-CD dissolved in PBS, were performed on a PerkinElmer LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer over an excitation range (250 nm to 500 nm).

Human RBC samples were obtained from blood of healthy donors after written consent at the Institute of Blood Transfusion and Hemobiology, Military Medical Academy, Belgrade, Serbia (ethical approvals: MMA No 43/2023, IMR No 149/2023). RBC were isolated from the buffy coats (blood fraction enriched in leucocytes, representing wasted material obtained after fractional centrifugation of whole blood, as described in Vučetić et al. [24] on the same day of collection by centrifugation at 1100 ×g for 20 min at 4 °C (Megafuge 1.0R, Heraeus centrifuge, Langenselbold, Germany). The supernatant, consisting of leucocytes in plasma, was carefully discarded by vacuum aspiration, and precipitated RBC were resuspended in isotonic saline solution (0.9 %, NaCl, Hemofarm, Vršac, Serbia) and washed with 0.9 % NaCl three times via centrifugation (at 1100 rpm for 15 min). Finally, the packed erythrocytes were resuspended in isotonic PBS (pH 7.2–7.4), counted, and adjusted to a concentration of 107 RBC/mL for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the hemocompatibility effect of N-CD on RBC

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the hemolytic effect, packed RBC were incubated with different concentrations of N-CD. For preparing negative and positive control samples, 0.1 mL of packed RBC (107/mL) suspension was mixed with 0.4 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) and 0.4 mL of distilled water, respectively. A 0.1 mL sample of packed RBC was diluted to 0.4 mL N-CD solution at varied concentrations (25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) prepared immediately before the experiment. For the treatment of RBC in this study, N-CD concentrations were selected based on those wildly used in comparable studies on the biocompatibility of nanoparticles, including carbon dots [18]. All samples were incubated at 37 °C for the indicated time periods (2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 16 h, 24 h) in the dark. After the incubation, samples were centrifuged at 10000 g for 10 min at 4 °C, and supernatants were collected and transferred to a 96-well plate. Hemolysis is defined as the destruction of RBC followed by release of hemoglobin, which can be detected in the supernatant of treated samples. UV–visible spectroscopy (Rayto RT-6100, China) was used to measure hemoglobin release by measuring the absorbance at 540 nm (A540) of the supernatant obtained after centrifugation of erythrocyte suspension incubated with N-CD [25]. The percentage of RBC hemolysis was calculated using the following formula: hemolysis % = (sample A540 − negative control A540) / (positive control A540 − negative control A540)) × 100.

To evaluate RBC deformability, 5 μL of packed RBC after incubation with PBS or treated with various concentrations of N-CD (25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) were resuspended in 600 μL of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Sigma-Aldrich Co., St. Louis, MO, USA). The resulting dispersion was introduced into the microchannel cuvette of RheoSCAN-AnD300 ektacytometer (RheoMeditech, Korea). All measurements were conducted at 37 °C. The deformability curve illustrates the mechanical properties of erythrocytes by showing how deformation changes under varying levels of externally applied shear stress (SS, Pa). The elongation index (EI) is calculated using diffraction images at specific shear stress values by device software.

The osmotic fragility of human RBC was determined by the Beutler method, adapted for experiments performed in 96 well microplates [26]. Briefly, packed RBC were incubated with PBS (control) or treated with various concentrations of N-CD solution for 2 h at 37 °C in the dark. After centrifugation, the collected cells were diluted 10-fold in 0.9 % NaCl. The assay was performed in 96 well, U-bottom microtiter plates. Control and treated packed erythrocytes (20 μL per well) in triplicate were added to wells containing decreasing concentrations of NaCl solution (0.9 %, 0.7 %, 0.6 %, 0.55 %, 0.5 %, 0.4 %, 0.3 %; 200 μL per well) or distilled water. The plates were gently mixed and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, the plates were centrifuged at 2500 rpm for 10 min, and 150 μL of supernatant was collected and transferred to flat-bottom microplates. The optical density at 540 nm (OD540 value, maximum absorption of oxyhemoglobin) of the supernatants was measured using a microplate reader (Rayto RT-6100, China). The extent of hemolysis (% HE) was calculated using the following equation:

The osmotic fragility data were plotted from the % HE values and fitted using a Boltzmann sigmoidal function in R (version 4.4.2). From the plotted curve, the concentration of NaCl solution that induced 50 % of maximal hemolysis (H50 values) was determined.

More detailed insight into the morphology of RBC was obtained using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). Briefly, packed RBC in PBS and RBC treated with N-CD (200 μg/mL) were incubated 2 h at 37 °C in the dark. The samples were then deposited on polycarbonate membrane filters with a diameter of 13 mm and a pore size of 0.1 μm (SPI Supplies, USA), fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution in PBS for 1 h, and washed twice in PBS. The preparations were post-fixed in 2 % osmium tetroxide for 1 h, rinsed in PBS, dehydrated through a graded series of ethanol (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 95 % and 100 %) for 10 min each, and then subjected to critical point drying using liquid carbon dioxide (Bal-Tec CPD030 Critical Point Dryer) [27]. After the deposition of a gold/palladium thin layer on the samples' surface, the samples were visualized using FE-SEM (TESCAN MIRA 3 XMU), in a vacuum at room temperature, with an acceleration voltage of 10 keV. The elemental composition of the samples was analyzed using energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS, Oxford Aztec X-max).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial interaction of N-CD by RBC
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescent microscopy analyses, packed RBC incubated with N-CD (200 μg/mL) were separated by centrifugation from the supernatant, which contained unbound N-CD. B-510LD4-SA LED fluorescence microscope with 100× objective was used to observe both RBC and RBC incubated with N-CD. The excitation light of 460 nm and 510LP emission filter were used to collect the fluorescence. An exposure of 100 ms was used for single acquisition images with OPTIKA CB CMOS camera. All images are processed with the ImageJ plugin for pseudo-flat field correction to account for uneven illumination. Synchronous fluorescence spectra (SPS) of N-CD hydrocolloids, as well as of control RBC and RBC incubated with N-CD dissolved in PBS were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer over an excitation range [250 nm to 500 nm] and synchronous wavelength intervals (Δλ) from 30 nm to 200 nm. All measurements were performed in right-angle geometry with a scan speed of 100 nm/min and a slit size of 10 nm. The effect of N-CD (200 μg/mL) on the absorption spectra (wavelength range between 390 nm and 750 nm) of hemoglobin (10 μM) was analyzed with Ultrospec 3300 pro UV-VIS spectrophotometer (Amersham Biosciences, Little Chalfont, UK) and measurements were performed in 1 cm quartz cuvettes (Hellma, USA)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of RBC morphology and intracellular ROS production
ce:section.ce:para: The size and surface granularity of RBC were assessed by flow cytometry using forward scatter (FSC) and side scatter (SSC) at 488 nm, respectively. A total of 10,000 events per sample were analyzed using a flow rate of 0.2 μL/s [28]. Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) production was determined by measuring the green fluorescence intensity emitted by the non-selective redox-sensitive dye 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCF-DA), able to detect various ROS. The production of reactive nitrogen species (RNS) was measured using NO/ONOO− sensitive fluorochrome 4-amino-5-methylamino-2′,7′-difluororesceinn (DAF), while the superoxide anion (O2 •−) generation was measured using dihydroethidium (DHE), exactly as previously described [29,30]. All samples (packed RBC in PBS or RBC treated with N-CD solution at various concentrations) were incubated at 37 °C, for 6 h in the dark. Briefly, DCF-DA (10 μM, Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) or DHE (20 μM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) were added to all RBC samples at the beginning, whereas DAF (5 μM, Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was added 30 min prior to the end of treatment. After washing the cells in PBS, the median intensity of green (FL1-DCF-DA and DAF) or red fluorescence (FL2-DHE), corresponding to total ROS/RNS production or superoxide levels, respectively, was determined using a FACSCalibur flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, Heidelberg, Germany) and CellQuest software for acquisition and analysis.

The statistical significance of the differences for flow cytometry data was analyzed in R (version 4.4.2) using Friedman test. A p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant. Data are presented as mean ± SD unless otherwise stated.

The results of the physicochemical characterization of the prepared N-CD used in the present study are presented in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a, the AFM image of the N-CD, spin-coated onto Si substrates from aqueous dispersion, reveals that the particles are 0.5 nm to 1 nm in height. On the other hand, the analysis of the AFM image of N-CD dispersed in PBS (Fig. 1c), reveals the size distribution of particles with heights ranging from 25 to 70 nm. This indicates limited aggregation of the N-CD after dispersion in the physiological buffer, PBS, which is essential for the subsequent application of N-CD to the RBC samples. Results obtained from dynamic light scattering analysis are shown in Fig. 1e. The size distribution of the synthesized N-CD dispersed in the PBS is bimodal with 92.1 % of the particles in the 68 ± 8 nm range, indicating an almost monodisperse size distribution of N-CD. Also, the presence of larger particles around 200 nm in size can be observed, resulting in polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.383. To examine the surface charge of the N-CD, the values of zeta potential (ζ) were determined at physiological pH (7.2–7.4) after dispersion in PBS. The particles exhibited negative surface charges with a ζ value of −16.2 ± 0.9 mV, indicating relatively good colloidal stability. It is important to note that due to the reported ζ value, the N-CD could readily interact with components of the biological environment. The negative surface charge can be attributed to the presence of deprotonated functional groups on the surfaces of the N-CD, such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups. The photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra of the N-CD in the visible region (Fig. 1f) showed the emission maximum at 520 nm upon excitation with a wavelength of 410 nm. This green fluorescence of the N-CD is related to the transitions from the surface states associated with the carbonyl group [23]. The characteristic Raman and FTIR spectra of the N-CD are presented in Supplementary materials Figs. S1 and S2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of N-CD on RBC morphology
ce:section.ce:para: As the exposure of RBC to nanomaterials may induce morphological aberrations indicating a loss of function, we studied the effect of N-CD on the morphology of RBC. The RBC incubated with PBS (control) or N-CD nanoparticles (25–200 μg/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C in the dark exhibit identical, intact morphology when analyzed using phase contrast microscopy (see Supplementary material Fig. S3). More detailed insights into RBC morphology obtained by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) also confirmed there was no effect on the morphology of RBC after N-CD treatment at the highest concentration (200 μg/mL), i.e., N-CD/RBC displayed a normal biconcave disc shape morphology characterized by a smooth and concave surface as the control RBC (Fig. 2 a, b.) Moreover, no RBC agglomeration was observed after N-CD treatment, which is an important indicator of their biocompatibility on RBC, knowing that agglomeration can affect blood flow properties and overall circulation. Comparable results were obtained using AFM analysis, which confirmed that RBC morphology was largely preserved, despite the interaction occurring at the cellular surface. Namely, AFM demonstrated the interfacial interaction of N-CD with the surface of RBC, as illustrated in the three-dimensional (3D) and two-dimensional (2D) topographic images of RBC treated with N-CD in Fig. 2d and e. As shown in the corresponding height profile in Fig. 2d, the RBC surface after N-CD treatment was corrugated. This represents a significant difference compared to RBC in control sample, whose surface is smooth, as shown in Fig. 2c. To further clarify the origin of the observed corrugations, enlarged topographic images of the inset in Fig. 2d are depicted in Fig. 2e (3D topography and 2D topography). These images revealed the surface of RBC together with adsorbed N-CD. The corresponding height profile in Fig. 2e shows that the height of the nanoparticles ranges from several tenths of nanometers to around hundreds of nanometers. Therefore, the estimated size of the nanoparticles corresponds well to the diameter of N-CD in the PBS, used as a medium for RBC treatment (approximately 60 nm) (Fig. 1e). Due to the negative zeta potential of N-CD, electrostatic interaction between N-CD and RBC is less likely [16]. Therefore, we hypothesize that the N-CD are adsorbed onto the RBC membranes primarily through hydrophobic interactions between the hydrophobic regions of the N-CD (aromatic ring) and the lipid bilayer of the RBC membrane, without affecting the RBC membrane structure or integrity. These results are consistent with the findings of Lin et al. [30], who indicated that size plays a crucial role in nanoparticle-RBC interactions, as smaller nanoparticles (5 nm) were able to penetrate RBC, while larger nanoparticles (100 nm) remained localized on the RBC surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of interfacial interaction between N-CD and RBC
ce:section.ce:para: Inherent green fluorescence of the N-CD allowed further analyses of the uptake of the N-CD by the RBC. The fluorescence spectra of the supernatants obtained after centrifugation of the control RBC and RBC samples incubated with N-CD (N-CD/RBC) were compared to the spectrum of N-CD dispersion in PBS at the same initial particle concentration, as shown in Fig. 3a. The intensity of the N-CD/RBC fluorescence in the supernatant, centered around 520 nm (410 nm excitation), is lower than the intensity of the corresponding band of the N-CD dispersed in PBS. This difference reflects the presence of interfacial phenomena between N-CD and RBC, including probably both internalization and adsorption. The interaction was further confirmed by analyzing the fluorescence of the RBC and NCD/RBC pellet (Fig. 3b). The treatment of RBC by N-CD resulted in pronounced fluorescence of the cells at both 410 nm and 430 nm excitation wavelengths, whereas the untreated RBC did not emit fluorescence in the investigated spectral range.

RBC treated with N-CD (NCD/RBC) were further analyzed using fluorescence microscopy. In Fig. 3c, fluorescent images of control RBC and NCD/RBC are presented, with additional fluorescence images provided in the Supplementary material (Figs. S4, S5, S6). In contrast to the untreated RBC, the N-CD/RBC exhibited an increase in fluorescence upon excitation at 460 nm which is localized within the cell region. In accordance with the previous reports on fluorescence emission of CD-treated RBC [32], our cost-effective N-CD, produced by a facile one-step-hydrothermal process, hold a promise to be an effective, non-harmful bio-labelling agent for RBC. Recent studies on N-CD use as bilirubin sensors [33] (bilirubin is a heme protein produced by hemoglobin degradation in aged RBC) support our investigation into expanding biomedical applications of a simple and eco-friendly produced N-CD reported in this study.

Additionally, the N-CD interaction with RBC was analyzed by complementary synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (SFS) (Fig. 4 ). The maps of the fluorescence intensities in the excitation wavelength range of λexc = (250 nm, 500 nm) and Δλ = (30 nm, 200 nm) synchronous interval range for the control RBC and N-CD/RBC sample are shown in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. The synchronous fluorescence map of the N-CD/RBC sample differs significantly from the corresponding map of the control RBC. The most illustrative differences between the samples occur at 270 nm, 330 nm, and 430 nm excitations, for which the emission spectra are presented in Fig. 4c. In the case of λexc = 270 nm excitation, the fluorescence spectrum of RBC shows peak emission at ∼340 nm that shifts to higher wavelengths in the presence of the N-CD. The fluorescence in the 270 nm/350 nm excitation/emission range originates from hemoglobin, specifically from the tryptophan and tyrosine residues in the protein [34]. Out of the two fluorescent amino acids, the fluorescence of tryptophan is more pronounced. Tryptophan residues are sensitive to the polarity of the environment and experience a bathochromic shift when transferred from a non-polar to a polar environment [34,35]. Consequently, the observed shift indicates that the tryptophan residues are more exposed to the polar environment in the presence of N-CD due to possible induction of hemoglobin disaggregation, as reported by Yang et al [36] This suggests the involvement of either hydrogen bonds or stacking interaction between N-CD and amino acid moieties [23]. Nevertheless, the fluorescence of the N-CD/RBC sample observed for the 330 nm and 430 nm excitations originate from the carbon dots, since UV/Vis spectrum showed the intactness of hemoglobin molecules after N-CD treatment (Supplementary material Fig. S7). The fluorescence observed for λexc = 330 nm excitation arises from the intrinsic C and graphitic N levels of the N-CD, while the previously discussed 430 nm/520 nm band comes from electronic transitions to the surface states of N-CD [23].

Many factors are responsible for the hemolytic or toxic effects of nanoparticles including nanoparticle shape, size, surface charge and modification. The biocompatibility of N-CD with red blood cells was evaluated by analyzing the hemorheological characteristics of human RBC, including hemolysis, osmotic fragility, deformability, aggregation, and morphological changes.

The RBC cells were incubated with PBS (as a negative control) or H2O (as a positive control) and treated with N-CD nanoparticles (25–200 μg/mL) for 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 16 h and 24 h at 37 °C in the dark. The photograph of RBC after 2 h of exposure to N-CD at different concentrations is shown in Fig. 5a. When RBC were treated by N-CD, free hemoglobin was not observed in supernatant, even at the highest N-CD concentration of 200 μg/mL, indicating that hemolysis did not occur. As shown in Fig. 5b, N-CD at all concentrations exhibited comparable or even lower hemolysis compared to RBC in PBS (negative control) at all tested periods. Namely, after 24 h, spontaneous hemolysis was more pronounced in the negative control than in the cells treated with N-CD, especially at the concentration of 200 μg/mL, suggesting a significant protective effect against spontaneous hemolysis. Interestingly, this result was opposite to comprehensive study on CD hemocompatibility of Li et al. [16] demonstrating dose- and time-dependent effects on RBC by CD (diameter < 10 nm) synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization of α-cyclodextrin. In particular as reported by Li et al. [16], CD concentrations of 0.01 mg/mL and 0.1 mg/mL preserved the physiological RBC morphology, while higher concentrations (from 1 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL) induced an increasing degree of RBC deformation and hemolysis. On the other hand, the functional properties of our N-CD on RBC lysis, i.e., neglected hemolysis, could be compared with the results of the minimal hemolytic effect of hyperbranched polyglycerol conjugated fluorescent CD [37], as well as photoluminescent eco-friendly synthesized CD from sugarcane molasses [38] and traditional Chinese medicine [39]. Additionally, Wasowicz et al. [40] reported excellent compatibility of detonated nanodiamonds (ND) with RBC, demonstrating that neither commercial unmodified, oxygenated (hydrophilic), nor hydrogenated (hydrophobic) ND induced any hemolytic activity. The variability in the hemocompatibility response of tested nanoparticles can be explained by the influence of their physicochemical properties (size, surface charge) and surface functionalization resulting from the synthesis and purification processes [41].

Moreover, the effects of N-CD were further evaluated on human monocytes to provide a more comprehensive understanding of their biocompatibility. After 24 h of treatment with the N-CD (25–200 μg/mL), cell viability was determined using MTT test. The results demonstrated that the viability of monocytes remained above 90 % even at higher doses, indicating minimal toxicity and good biocompatibility of N-CD (see Supplementary material Fig. S8).

Additionally, we tested the effect of N-CD on RBC osmotic fragility using one of the most suitable procedures for determining the resistance of the erythrocyte membrane to hemolysis under conditions of osmotic stress. Representative osmotic fragility curves of RBC treated with N-CD after 2 h are presented in Fig. 4c. The curves were obtained by fitting the experimental data using a Boltzmann sigmoidal function. The results revealed that treatment with N-CD (25–200 μL/mg) had no effects on RBC osmotic fragility, as the calculated H50 values for all concentrations evaluated (range 0.51–0.54) were within the range for healthy RBC (range 0.44–0.54). Minor discrepancies between different concentrations of N-CD were observed only at 0.3 % NaCl, indicating a potential effect of 200 μg/mL N-CD on the measured optical density, i.e. the maximal extent of hemolysis due to possible interactions with released hemoglobin molecules (Hb). Specifically, UV–Vis spectra of 200 μg/mL N-CD incubated in a hemoglobin solution (10 μM) demonstrated overlapping absorption bands and superimposition of the resulting absorbance (Supplementary material Fig. S7).

We next investigated whether treatment with N-CD might impact RBC deformability, an important biophysical property that allows RBC to deform and pass through capillaries to deliver oxygen to tissues [42]. The resulting deformability curves, presented in Fig. 5d, illustrate a comparative analysis of erythrocytes between control samples and RBC samples treated with various concentrations of N-CD (25–200 μg/mL). Surprisingly, treatment with N-CD at all concentrations resulted in a slight increase in the calculated elongation index (EI) at 3 Pa (3 Pa is an average pressure in microvasculature), compared to naïve RBC, indicating that N-CD treatment has a positive effect, i.e. increase RBC deformability and viscoelasticity. Limited literature is available on the deformability of RBC following treatment with CD. However, studies involving ND have shown that while hemolysis was not observed, RBC deformability significantly decreased at the highest concentration of ND used [31], which is in contrast with our findings. Since RBC deformability depends on hemoglobin oxygenation state [31], it can be speculated that newly prepared N-CD affect oxygen binding or penetration through the RBC membrane. Future studies on N-CD, particularly those exploring oxygenated and deoxygenated states of RBC, are necessary to better understand these effects.

It should also be considered that RBC do not exhibit phagocytosis or endocytosis; suggesting that alternative mechanisms are involved when RBC are exposed to nanoparticles [43]. These mechanisms may include diffusion, transmembrane channels, and adhesive interactions through electrostatic, Van der Waals and steric forces. Based on the results of this study, which show no significant changes in RBC morphology, deformability, or hemolytic activity following exposure to the synthesized N-CD, we hypothesize that spatial interactions between the N-CD and the RBC surface are responsible for the observed effects. This aligns with previous reports which suggest that smaller nanoparticles (around 5 nm) are generally more biocompatible and can penetrate biological membranes more effectively due to their higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, avoiding physical damage to RBC membranes and reducing hemolytic activity. In contrast, larger N-CD (>50 nm) may negatively affect biocompatibility, by localizing on the surface of the RBC potentially leading to membrane disruption or RBC aggregation, which could increase hemolysis [40]. However, our results demonstrate that N-CD, regardless of size, did not significantly affect RBC morphology, aggregation, or hemolysis, suggesting that size did not influence their hemocompatibility.

Although RBC compatibility is crucial for biomedical applications of N-CD, only a few studies have examined their hemolytic activity, effects on morphology, and deformability. The advantage of N-CD over regular CD likely lies in their surface chemistry - specifically, nitrogen doping introduces amino groups with a positive charge at physiological pH, unlike CD with negatively charged carboxyl groups. This alteration may reduce harmful interactions with RBC, as certain functional groups are known to decrease hemolytic activity [44]. Additionally, the enhanced fluorescence of N-CD could improve biocompatibility by enabling real-time visualization and assessment of interactions with RBC membranes. This capability could help identify safer profiles for N-CD compared to regular CD. However, direct comparisons of hemolytic activity between the N-CD and regular CD remain limited, and further research is needed [4,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of N-CD on induction of RBC oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: The flow cytometric analysis was conducted to investigate the effects of N-CD on RBC morphology at a single cell level and to determine if there was any intracellular oxidative stress induction within RBC by N-CD. This analysis involved examining various parameters, including cell size (Forward Scatter - FSC) and granularity/cell shape (Side Scatter - SSC). Upon treatment with N-CD at all concentration, flow cytometry results did not show an increase in both FSC and SSC values after 6 h in comparison with control (RBC incubated with PBS) cells (Supplementary material Fig. S9). The results suggest that RBC morphology remained unaffected with no alterations in the size and granularity compared to untreated controls. These findings align with SEM and AFM results, which also indicated a lack of notable modifications of RBC topology. Furthermore, flow cytometric analysis was used to assess the effects of N-CD on the intracellular production of ROS/RNS in RBC, as a marker of oxidative stress. It is known that some nanoparticles, upon attachment to the RBC membrane and subsequent cellular uptake, can immediately trigger an increase in ROS production, lipid oxidation and activation of antioxidant enzymes, leading to the induction of oxidative stress [41]. Another implication of the disturbance in RBC redox status may relate to the previously suggested N-CD effect on the oxygenation state of hemoglobin molecules, leading to the formation of pro-oxidative hemoglobin dimers. The analysis of ROS, RNS and superoxide anion production using redox-sensitive fluorochromes DCF-DA, DAF and DHE, revealed no significant increase in fluorescence intensity (no statistical significance), suggesting that despite of results of PL which showed RBC interaction with N-CD there was no corresponding increase in ROS/RNS production in the RBC treated with N-CD after 6 h of treatment (Fig. 6 a, b, c).

In conclusion, we demonstrate that the newly synthesized “green” N-CD exhibit excellent biocompatibility with human RBC, showing a protective effect against RBC hemolysis, no impact on osmotic fragility, and even increased RBC deformability at all tested concentrations. Although the AFM and PL results confirmed that N-CD have interfacial contact with RBC, they do not significantly affect RBC aggregation or morphology, nor do they induce oxidative stress. The reported data suggests that synthesized, cost-effective N-CD are safe and promising candidates for biomedical applications as fluorescent agents for RBC labelling. However, further research is needed to gain deeper insights into the interaction between N-CD with all blood components, to assess their behavior in physiological environments (such as the corona effect), and to identify any potential adverse effects.

Biljana Ristić: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Đorđe Trpkov: Resources, Investigation. Radovan Dojčilović: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Tamara Đukić: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Dušan K. Božanić: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Radoš Stefanović: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Borislav Vasić: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ivana Drvenica: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.
All authors of the manuscript agree to its submission and publication.

The collection of venous blood from healthy blood donors at the Institute for Transfusiology and Hemobiology, Military Medical Academy, Belgrade (MMA), Serbia and the Institute for Medical Research- University of Belgrade (IMR) was approved by the Institutional Ethical Review Board (MMA No 43/2023, IMR No 149/2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors did not use AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process.

The authors acknowledge funding provided by the Institute for Medical Research, University of Belgrade, (contract No. 451-03-136/2025-03/200015), the Institute of Physics Belgrade, and Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences (contract No. 451–03–136/2025–03/200017), through the grants from the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared that no competing interest exists. All authors of the manuscript agree to its submission and publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Jelena Pajović, Faculty of Physics, University of Belgrade for her assistance with fluorescent microscope analysis and Dragana Tošić, Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences - National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, University of Belgrade for her help with the AFM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioadv.2025.214296.
